speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This conversation with Ida Mielke Newman took place at her home at Cameron on February 18, 1977. The interviewer was Sam Schrager.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What were your people first coming here?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: They came here in 1901. I was three years old.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And where was it they came from?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: From Minnesota. Oh, there were quite a number of families came here from Minnesota. Reverend Hupp was their minister in Minnesota. And he moved to Cameron and he told his former congregation how wonderful country this was. So I don't know how many families, seven or eight families came through him. They were members of his congregation.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where in Minnesota?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: From Arlington and New Auburn. Were your parents born in Germany before— My mother was born in Germany and my father was born in Minnesota. My mother was four years old and her parents came across.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So then your father and mother both probably spoke English pretty well, huh?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: My dad did, but my mother spoke broken English. Because in Minnesota where they lived that was all German. Everyone talked German. And even here when I was a youngster everyone talked German. I couldn't talk English when I started to school.

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Any other things you'd like to— Oh yes, I'm just getting started.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When they came, your parents, did they have much?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Oh no, people were very poor.

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: My dad bought 160 acres and I don't know what he paid down, whether it was $1 ,000 or $2 ,000. And that's all they had. Of course they brought along goods.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they have much of an outfit to bring?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: No. As far as the machinery is concerned, no. It's just their clothes. And I think they brought some of their household goods.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they come with other families at the same time?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Yes. My grandparents came at the same time. And I don't know just what other families. And then some other families came the next year in 1902. The Harthold and the Milpies came in 1901. And the Wagoners and the Reblecs and Brontics. I don't know why I was trying to think of some more names that came the next year.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you came, were you old enough to remember that at all?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: I remember very, very little. See, I was only four. I wasn't four, I was only three. And I don't know if I remembered or whether I just remembered from here, see. You know, a great many times they'll talk about it if you think that you remember.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they say it was rough at all?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Or was it just an easy, they took the train I remember.

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: They took the train and when my mother, my mother always said when she came to Kendrick, she says, oh my goodness, she says how in the world are we ever going to get out of this hole?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: She said Minnesota was everything was level. She's going to say how they could ever get out of it. She says, there can't be any farming in a country like this. You started with disappointment then. Well, she wasn't really disappointed because she knew my dad wouldn't buy something that he couldn't take care of.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was he out here before she?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Yes. Yes, he was out here. And I remember he was in the hospital. And I bought the farm before she came out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How many brothers and sisters?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Let's see, there were seven of us there. There were three brothers born in Minnesota and my sister and I were born in Minnesota. And then I had two brothers were born in Cameron. And the place that my father bought was the old Cameron place where Cameron was named after. See, Mr. Cameron had the first post office. And he was quite a prominent man. And he was a county commissioner or something, a Lewisman.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was he German too?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: No. Didn't sound like him. No, he wasn't German. I don't know what his point is.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did the community start off then, not as a German community?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: No, it didn't start as a German community. So it became that way. It became that way through the church, I think. The church was built then in Cameron and through the church, the Lewisman Church, the people came and settled here. And it was mostly through that that they settled right around Cameron.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did the church draw in a big area, you think, to start with?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: No, just from Cameron.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it just from where?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Just Cameron, yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was Hough the first minister?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: No. No, it's in the book for the first time. Because I know Hattie Johnson told me the first minister was from Germany, he and his wife. And she had a difficult time adapting, she said, to this country. I see. To get used to. Yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, when you were old enough to start remembering things, what was it like around here when you were a kid?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Well, there were lots and lots of trees. The contour of the country seemed different. Because people had to—well, they had to make a living. You couldn't go to a store and buy things. Just, you know, you're very necessities. And you had to have your own cattle, had your own fruit in your garden, and raised your own things. Your chickens and your pigs that you put your in the fall and cured your meat. And that was your meat supply for the year. And your chickens. And your canned your fruit and your meat, sauerkraut, and your potatoes and apples. So many apples that you stored in the winter. And you made your own butter.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it densely settled then?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Yes, much more so than now. The farms were 40 acres, 80 acres, and 160 acres was a fairly good size farm. And people, farms with horses, usually had four horses and a foot burner, and a cedar, and a taro, foot burner—no, no, foot burner, just one flout. And then that's about all they had.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How much neighboring? Was there a lot?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Yes, there was a lot of neighboring. People visited back and forth. You'd order to some people's place on Sunday for dinner, and you'd eat what they had. And you were very welcome. And people visited back and forth a lot. And then the evenings, a lot of times, the men would get together and play cards or tell stories, stories handed down from generation to generation.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Stories really from the old country born?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Yes, from the old country, yes. And of course, my parents know stories from Minnesota. Well, you say they were handed down.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I don't expect you to remember the stories themselves, but what kind of things would they be likely to be about?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Well, one thing that I remember—I don't remember the story itself, but my grandmother, because she was born in 1827, she lived with us because her grandfather had passed away, was telling about spooks. For a century, we don't believe in it now. She didn't believe in it either, but she was telling about it. Oh, we thought those stories were wonderful. And then they used to tell about some different people, about different things that they did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You don't remember what she had to say about spooks?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: No, I wish they were going to say I could. Because I bet that would be fun to— Yes. And when she was in Germany, they lived—they were in Prussian. They were near the Polish border. And I remember her teaching me some Polish. And, oh, I wish I could remember it. I can remember Jan Waczy, one, two, three, and Demi Nusz, and Jan Dore Brajczyk. And that's as much as I can remember. And I wish I could remember more.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, there was an awful lot of German spoken here at that time, though, huh?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was most of the talking in German in the houses?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Yes. The talking was all in German.

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: And, oh, I don't know when we really started talking English. I suppose when the children started to go to school, when after I got older, we had dances. And we had dances, and we'd have parties. And Sundays, we'd go over to somebody's house in the afternoon. Young people would meet. And we'd talk, and we'd play games, and we just had a lot of fun. And then the celebrations were now—when I was a youngster, where the Fourth of July, they'd meet at some old—in some grove. Or some people's places in the pasture, they'd have a nice, mean pasture. They'd play cards—not play cards, but to play games baseball and races. Uh -huh.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And— Did you play—were there outdoor games that you used to play, like circle games and that kind of stuff?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Well, yeah, we used to play.

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: We'd drop the handkerchief, then the winter fox and goose. And I was trying to think of some other games. Oh, there are different games that we play. We used to play quite a bit of baseball.

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: You say dances. Now, dancing was quite acceptable. Nobody objected to having dances inside around here. No. No. Some places I know they didn't fly. Yes, no. No, they didn't.

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Of course, there were certain people that, you know, was against their religion and so on. They didn't go to dances, but most of them did. And it was, of course, right here in Cameron when we had the dances, it was just the home group. Of course, they had some friends at Leland. That's about two miles away. That's about as far as we got.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Dances at homes or in the school?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: No, in the homes. At first, we had them in the homes. And then after a while, there was a hall at Cameron. It used to be the old post office. We used to have them in there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did it be in the whole neighborhood or just a few neighborhoods?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Well, it was in the neighborhood and then a few of the young people around. And then another thing we had when I went to school was literary societies. And they were a lot of fun. They had debates, old recitations, and singing. And one of the things that was a lot of fun, they had a paper called the Hogg's Eye. And it would be some stories made up of people in the neighborhood. So they always thought that was a lot of fun. I wonder why the Hogg's Eye. That's kind of an amusing name for it. Well, it was supposed to be amusing. It was supposed to be, you know, just for fun. Do you know, it was funny jokes. Yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would the stories be true?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: No, no, they wouldn't be true. No, they were just for fun.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you had, say, like debates, would that be taking two sides of a question or something?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Yes, yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would they be serious or would they be serious?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: No, they were real serious debates.

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: I remember one of the jokes at the Hogg's Eye.

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: They said one of the men at the table, someone asked one of the children at school, what does your father do when you sit down, before you sit down, when you sit down to eat before you take your food?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Well, he says, my father says, go easy with the butter, kids. It's 15 cents a pound.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It was supposed to be grace, right?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Yes, it was supposed to be grace, but that's what the youngster, that's what the youngster says his father said. That's a little different than now, the price.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about, say, like the debates? Do you remember any of the kind of, the sort of thing that you say serious, the sort of thing that people would talk about? Would it be politics or religion?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Well, no, it'd be politics and different subjects. I wish I could remember some of what I don't. It doesn't really matter. I know, when I went to school as in a first debate, I was in, I don't know what grade I was, it must have been about the sixth, fifth or sixth. And we had, the topic was resolved that the print, that having the printing press was much more important than the discovery of America. And I was on the printing press side, and my teacher was printing the paper. So, of course, I thought I had a real, real good point. So I said, well, if the printing press wouldn't have been founded, why, my teacher couldn't have printed his paper. And so, when the opponent got up, he said, no, he says he couldn't have printed his paper, but his paper isn't any good anyway. So that was the end of our debates.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The end of all the debates?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Yes, the teacher didn't let us have any more debates.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Because of that?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: I don't know, I imagine so. Or if we did have, I don't remember.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he put out the camera and paper? Did he make any money, you think, doing that?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Oh, not too much. Just sort of on the side.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that paper in German at the time?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: No, oh no, that was English. No, that was when we were going to English school.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, was the paper for the whole community?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Yes, yeah, whoever subscribed for it, just the same as any other paper. And it was just news from the, from Cameron and the surrounding territories. It was just a little pamphlet.

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Well, I'm real curious about some of the customs that, when you were young, that people still kept, that were from the old country.

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Sort of things that have died out since then. Well, the celebration of Christmas, and giving Christmas dinners, and family together at Christmas, is one of the, I think was handed down.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that, was that really, was it family or was it a community celebration?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Family, family. That was a family celebration.

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Is that the, see I was trying to think of some community celebrations. We used to have picnics. I mean, that's about one. And then one of the biggest things was on Christmas Eve to go to church, and we, children would have new dresses and new clothes. And that was one of the big things in the community.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You would go to church Christmas Eve, but the, was there a celebration at church besides the service?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: That was all at home. That was all, yes. We didn't have any celebrations then. You know, we have now, more so now than they used to years ago.

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: I know Frank Brocky, you probably know him. Yes, I know him. He told me, he told me how his father used to always dress up as Santa Claus and come in, and come in. And the kids were always scared of him. I mean, they always were sure he was Santa Claus, even though it was their father. They couldn't recognize him when he was a kid.

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: So we, we always got some gifts at Christmas, but not, never Santa Claus. They told Santa Claus to come with a sleigh. And of course there's always one little thing, but not really getting very many things, because people just couldn't afford it. And, of course the next morning we'd say, yeah, we heard Santa Claus come. And we decorated, one thing we did, we always decorated the tree. And they, everyone did, and they made a lot of their decorations. Once in a while we make them now to show people what kind of decorations we used to have. And then Easter, there's another thing they'd celebrate, you know, with Easter eggs. We had, they, we didn't have eggs all winter, saved up a few eggs so we could have some for Easter. And that's just kind of thing. Of course the Fourth of July celebration was always celebrated. But that would have to be an American one instead of the Jewish one. That's an American one. Yes, quite sure. I know, I was thinking what other holidays there were. Those, I think, were the traditional holidays.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I, now I've heard about how there was both a low German and a high German in the dialects?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Yes, not in talk both of them.

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: My dad and his mother taught low German, and we children taught low German to them. And my mother didn't talk low German, she talked the regular German, the high German, and we taught high German to her.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now what was the difference? I mean, why were there two different ones? It depends on where you come from?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Well, no, different, well, they had different dialects, you know, in Germany, the same as they have here. If you're from the south or from the north or from the west, you know, your dialects are a little bit different.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why did they call them one low and the other one?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: I don't know why it was called low, but the low German is not a written German. The high German, the regular German is a real German, and the low German is not a written German.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The regular German, is that what they used in the church?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Oh, yes.

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there a lot of difference between them?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Oh, yes, there's a lot of difference.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And I just kind of think, maybe you could give me a word, maybe I could tell you how, what is a high German or low German?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: It's a common word.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what about something like school? School? Schuyl? That's the in German?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: No, I don't know a lot of them, I suppose. We taught Schuyl two years.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Or like church?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: No, church, the scarce, and scarce in low German, but an I, like the pronoun I. Yes. In high German, it's ich, and in our low German, it's ich. Ich. Yes. Our low, it depends on sound. Uh -huh. And they didn't have them in the high German.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, could people, who could only speak one or the other, could they speak to each other easily and make themselves understood?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Well, they could make each other, yes.

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Yes. Oh, that was a difference. I think they could pretty well understand one another.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think there was much desire to become Americanized here?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Very, very much so. My grandfather, when my grandfather came from Germany, he became an American citizen, by the way, because he wanted to be an American citizen. And of course, then Civil War broke out, and he was in the Civil War in Schumann's March to the Sea.

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: And my folks always thought of being very good Americans. That's what they came to this country for, and that's where we had these Fourth of July celebrations. Well, there was a lot, a lot of patriotism in the show, and there's always someone, always had a speaker, to talk about the good of this country and different things. And then they usually ended up with the program with Singing America, and everyone sang and really nambered.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But say, like, from day to day, though, since this was mostly, it was mostly all the German community right here, right?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Yes.

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: So people probably didn't have too much contact with people that weren't German, at least when they were right here. Not right at Cameron, but if they did their trading at Kendrick, and that was English, they couldn't sell off. It just brought a community, right in itself in German, but as far as trading, and with Loosch and so on. And I know when the First World War broke out, they sold more war bonds than almost any community. They really supported that country.

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: It's funny, because I know I've heard, because I've heard that some people did, like Frank Brockie talked to the other people too, who were German, that told me that people got a hard time, because they were German during that time, with some people, some people getting a hard time.

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Yes, there are some people. That's one reason my mother never liked to talk much. When she was out, she had a broken English, and she had that accent. And it always bothered her. And nowadays, if she'd be talking now, I'd actually just write, it would make sense. But at that time, I heard that especially like Leland, there were people in Leland who really gave Cameron a hard time, because of it, during that time. Well, I can tell you something now, something that happened at the First World War. I'm not going to mention any names, because I wouldn't want to know what any names of the engine. Someone, the flag at the schoolhouse, wasn't taken down for some reason or other at night, and somebody came from there and took the flag and stomped down it to the ground to give the German people a bad name. Of course, that person has died and has moved away from here a long time ago, so I hope that I don't hurt any people. I've seen some, because I just love the brilliant people. I don't think he's got any ability to say anything anymore. And there was someone at the schoolhouse that night and saw this.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Saw it done?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Yes, and they weren't supposed to be there, so they couldn't say anything. Of course, I knew it at the time, but I never said anything, because I wasn't supposed to say anything. I wasn't going to tell and get someone into trouble. So those things did happen.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were they there on a date or something like that?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: At night. That's it. They were there on a date.

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Well, I think it was Hattie Johnson that told me that... Now, I don't know if you remember this happening, but she said that when boys were going off to war at one point, there was a minister from Kendrick that really gave them a hard time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember that?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: No, I don't. Although her brother went the same time my brothers went.

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: No, it could be. I think she said that there was a farewell dinner, and he said some things that really said, if I had any German blood, I would get rid of it, because I would be patriotic. Yes. My mother always taught it. It doesn't make any difference what color you are, or what creed, or what nationality, or how much education you have or how little education you have. It doesn't make any difference. It's what you do with yourself and do with the rest of the people. That's what comes. And so we always try to live by that. Oh, it seemed... When I heard what I heard, it seemed very unfair at that time. Yes, it was. Yes, there were incidents. I don't know of an incident right now, but I do know even in World War II, now all of my children were in, boys were in. One of them was a German prisoner. He was shot down over by a British garden. And another one was in Patton's army, and one boy was in Korea, and the daughter of a total nurse was training soon up through the services. They were all in, and I know that before the second boy went, and he was ahead of his age in school, that he was reported that he... that they were wondering why he wasn't in the army, and he was only 17 years old. So I know that those things happened.

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: It sounds like the business with war bonds. I heard that what I've heard elsewhere from people who aren't German is that they... They would keep... They expected German people to buy more than anybody else to prove that they were on the right side.

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Yes. Yes. And these people did. They did buy a lot of war bonds. And there may have been one or two families that were a little prejudiced. I don't know. Well, it still had ties to the old country. Sure. Well, they still had relatives. We still have some relatives there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know why people were leaving Germany at the time and coming over? Why they figured there was a better chance here?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: My grandfather from the milky side came over because he did not like all these wars. He saw so much fighting there. So that's why... One reason he came here, and he's the one that served in the Civil War. And then my grandfather, Hartung, from my mother's side... You see, there was such a class distinction in the old countries. My grandmother was from upper class, from well -educated people, and my grandfather was just more of a common person. And of course, you know, that was looked down upon. But he married your grandmother anyway. Yes, but he was a wonderful man, a wonderful broad -minded man, to tell the truth more so than my grandmother. Well, maybe it's because he was common instead of high. Yes, I think that had something to do with it. And that's one reason they came to the United States to America. Because of the class. Because of the class distinction. And then they thought they'd have a better opportunity here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: If he stayed in Germany, would that have really stopped him from rising in the social system?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Well, I don't know if you could rise in the social system very much there unless you're born to a social system there. And that's the way I think it used to be. But here, nothing stopped him. Do whatever he... No. Yes.

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Well, and that's my... From the milky side, my grandfather that came and fought in the Civil War. He was from these big landlords, his ancestors. And he never liked this distinction either. That's one person higher. Should be a higher class than another. Oh, I thought you said he was lower. No, but that was my other grandfather, the grandpa milky that was in the Civil War. Yes. The other one, he was from a higher station. Yes. So, and they... My family and ancestors didn't like that distinction. No.

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: That's interesting.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that would be true of a lot of the people that came, other people in this community too?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: I kind of think so.

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: I think so. Although I do think that there isn't much distinction now as there used to be. I don't know whether you found it that way or not. I don't know. For richer people. Maybe not class distinction as much as old financial. I think.

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: I think it's in a way, you could say that's replaced the class. No, it depends. If you have money, then you're...

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: That's good. I don't like that too much either. No, but I don't think it's as bad as it used to be. That I would know because I wasn't here. No, right here it isn't because there are people who have lots and lots of money, just as common and just the same as everybody else.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it like that in this community from the beginning? Was there much distinction?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: No, I don't think so because at the beginning nobody had any finances. Everyone had very little.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you think people were roughly equal?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Yes, they were roughly equal.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were there some people who tried to put on airs anyway?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Oh, once in a while. Once in a while. There are some. Not very many.

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: That would be kind of normal. Yes, it's normal. It's normal.

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: About like every place else I imagine. But they do help if there's any disaster. I think they're just the most wonderful people to help you out every way they can.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: In the early days, was there much need to do that to help each other or was it more of a family, each family for itself?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: No, I think they helped each other when they possibly could, especially if there were any illnesses. I think they did. I think out in the country there is more of, it isn't so much everybody for themselves as being kind of a big family. And if they needed help, why they'd come and help you.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Sickness, when you're talking about that, is there any times in particular that you're thinking of when there was sickness?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: I know like the flu was a pretty rough one at that time. Yes, and I know when our family had typhoid, nearly all the family had typhoid. My older brother was going to high school in Spokane. He couldn't go. Another one was going to Kendreton, had to stay out. My sister and I stayed out. We all had typhoid.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know where it came from?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: From a water supply. My older brother was throwing sacks. He had another man was throwing sacks and they had done him some bad water. Those days, if you had typhoid, you were sick for three weeks. I didn't have any antibiotics or anything to be a preventative.

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: My father and one brother and a grandmother didn't get it, but all the rest of us did. And before that there were several families had it. There was one family from Germany who lived here. They lost their two boys from typhoid fever. They buried a pair in the cemetery.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there fear in your family that not everybody would make it through that typhoid?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Well, we were hopeful that we wouldn't. One of my brothers died. He was a year and four months old and he got spinal meningitis. And typhoid, he died. And my uncle died.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: From typhoid? At that same time?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Yes, yes, same time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have neighbors help?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: No. There were enough of us. My grandmother didn't have it and my dad didn't have it that we could help each other. And of course you didn't want people to come in because you didn't want to give it to them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember being sick?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: I remember it very, very well. Because at that time all you got to eat is a little bit of three tablespoons of broth with every bit of fat removed three times a day and that's what you got to eat for three weeks. And then when your fever was gone, then you had to go back and just start gradually with food. And you lost so much weight there wasn't much left of you. I can believe it. Of course that was the proper treatment at that time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did your mother work hard?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Very, very hard. She worked very hard. She was the oldest woman of her brothers and sisters. And I think she was 19 when she was married. And there were ten children in the family and three of them passed away. And when they were children there were seven of us left. She was a wonderful, wonderful woman. And we all worked hard but we were all taught honest, to be honest.

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: And we were all taught, well, my folks sent us all to school. We could go on to school. There were very, very few children that even graduated from the eighth grade. They sent us to high school and we also went to college. And my mother and dad always taught us, better yourself, but never think that you're better or above anybody else.

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: It's interesting that they had those strong feelings.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I wonder, where do you think that, why do you think they even had those ideas at that time?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Well, I kind of think so because my mother's mother, her folks, were well educated, were very well educated and belonged to the high class in Germany. And her father was a lipperer class. And that's where she learned the class distinction. And she never liked it. It isn't what you were born to or how much money you have, it's what you do with yourself. And she was very, very strong in teaching that to her children. And so was my father.

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: She probably liked the American system pretty well since it's the democracy instead of the inheritance and that kind of thing.

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: That's right.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But for honesty, was that something that you think was true of the whole, that they taught, that all the kids were taught at that time? The stress on honesty, or you think that was special in your family? In our family?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: It was very special, I think. Although I think that most of the parents taught their children honesty.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When they taught you honesty, does that mean like never to lie in that sort of thing?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Well, not to tell lies to hurt anyone. Your little white lies wouldn't hurt anything. But to tell some lies that would be detrimental to someone else. Otherwise, what they call a little white lie, I suppose, it would help anyone. That wouldn't hurt anything. But we were always taught not to. And another thing we were always taught never to think that we were more than anyone else. Because everyone had their station in life.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, for like the work that your mother did, what were her responsibilities to the farm and to the family that she carried out when we were young?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Well, see, there wasn't much of it. Just had a little bit of a house to take.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Won't it be good to one another and do our work honestly and fairly?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did she work outside very much? Did she help your father?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Not too much. Well, probably milk cows and do something like that, but she never went out in the field and worked. It's usually the older boys that did. And of course then when they were big enough, they went off to high school and to college. And then the younger ones at work.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did your father have most of that land cleared when he got her? Did he clear it up when he got after?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: I wonder if it's only about half cleared. See, the country was new. That was in 1901 when my folks came from Minnesota. And it was quite a new country. And there were lots and lots of shrubs and brush and small trees. And so he just cleared up a lot, a lot of the land.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he work out or was he able to just get by right here?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Just worked out. No, just got by. Because at that time most of the people raised everything that they needed. All the bread, you know, the staples of bread and a few things like that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about say like hardest time? Did the men go outside to work?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: No. The way they had the hardest time, there used to be somebody had a thrashing machine and an engine. And then the farmers would help one another with the wagons and horses where they'd haul the grain to the... There's some pictures in the book about it. And they'd help each other out that way.

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: But it doesn't sound like there was much chance to get any hard cash. There wasn't.

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: There wasn't. There was very little hard cash. Because the only thing, well, you had some grain to sell, but you needed so much of it for your cattle and for your horses and for your hogs and chickens. And then, of course, there were lots and lots of fruit trees. People had lots of big orchards there at that time. And I remember when I was just a little youngster that my dad sold peach plums one year, put them to Kendrick, and I know they shipped them out somewhere. And the way they picked them, they put them right in the big wagon box. There was a great big box that hauled them to Kendrick. You know, everything's got to be wrapped and everything. And it seems to me he sold some pairs one year. They'd get a little money. Just to get a little money.

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Well, you say you got to go on to high school.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that at Kendrick?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: No. I went to high school in Spokane. My two brothers had graduated from high school in Spokane. I had an aunt living in Spokane. And so I stayed with her, and I went to high school there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What school?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Lewis and Clark High School. And then my brother was deputy assessor at Lewis and then I went to normal school at Lewis and stayed with two of my brothers then.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you learn teaching?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Yeah, I taught school.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You taught afterwards?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Afterwards, yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Ah, where did you teach?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: I taught middle school about three miles from here and then I taught near Melrose.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the first?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Middle school, about halfway between here and South Lake. My son owns that farm now. And then the first year I taught, I taught at Melrose. Near Melrose, near Giffords. In the Meijer district.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did you get very good training to teach or did you learn it in the classroom?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Well, we learned. We learned, of course, learning at school and actually teaching, those are two altogether different things, but you do have the fundamentals when you go. And I liked it very, very much. And if I'd have to do it over again, I think I'd like to do it again. And especially with the youngsters, small children.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was it like for you when you were first teaching? I mean, what kind of experience was it?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: I liked it very much, but I had a lot to learn too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And I only had, what, I had eleven pupils?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: I think I had six, six grades. And some of them only had one inner grade and we had to walk about a mile to school.

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: And I found it very, very interesting.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it very demanding?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: No, I don't think so. Well, you had to do all of your own work. You couldn't get seat work, you know, where they have a seat work, not for children. You can buy it and have it all prepared for you. Well, you had to prepare everything yourself. And I didn't find it demanding. And then there's one thing I was like, is to be with the children out in their playground and play with them. And see that it was all supervised.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you find that you were to each kid, that each kid was an individual to you?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Yes, very much so.

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Of course, in, uh, last year I taught school, I had all, I had, I had thirty -three pupils and I had all eight grades. Didn't give you much time for each class.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were there things that the kids really liked, that, you know, the things that were most popular with them that you, that you would do for them?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: One thing that I know that they liked, and I, I really liked too, on Friday afternoons after recess, I would have, oh, just a little speech. And I'd have the children tell some of their experiences. You know, and of course that would be, you know, it wasn't connected with their language, but they could learn through it. And that was one of the most interesting classes I think I ever had. And I don't know whether they always told me just exactly what they did or whether they just imagined some of it. I know one of the boys used to tell such big stories, and I think part of it was imagination. But then, sometimes those people that had that imagination can get the beer bikers and so on.

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: It sounds like they were having a chance to express themselves. Yes, yes. It was good.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that your idea?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: It sounded like you had to tell them. Yes, that was my idea.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have much of a role to play in the communities that you taught into?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Because I've heard that the teacher was a rather important person for the whole community, not just for the class. No, no, they, not at that time.

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Maybe more so, like, if you, like, your camera was a little pound, probably more so. But really there wasn't. Of course we had our programs and so on. But otherwise, it wasn't in the communities where I taught anyway. I think there were some communities where...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were you able to keep the money that you made from teaching, or did you use it to help the family out?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Well, I didn't have to help the family. I did owe them some. I had some debts when I started the school. The first year I taught, I had a seven -month school and got $60 a month. And I had some debts. My folks had lowered me so I could buy a watch so I'd have something to eat time and a few other odds and ends. Of course I paid it back.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But when you were in Spokane and in Lewiston, did you work out at all at that time?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: No.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Or just straight go to school?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: I just straight went to school and I stayed with my aunt. Although, of course, I helped take care of the children. They had four little children. And I helped with the housework.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that a public school, Lewis and Clark?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Yes, it was one of the big high schools. At that time, I guess they have lots of them in Spokane now. It was an exceptionally good school.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And most of the people in the community here didn't get a chance to do that, to go on?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: No, I think our family and the Wagner family were about the only ones at that time. Of course, now they all go to school. But at that time, I think we were about the only ones. Well, my dad always said he could have had quite a few offices and jobs in Minnesota, but he didn't have a schooling. And so he says he was going to see to it that his children got to go to school. And so my mother and dad sacrificed a lot so we could go to school.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: In what way?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Well, they were poor, very poor. And a lot of boys stayed home, and they could buy big farms and everything. Of course, my dad said, I never have much land. But he says, you children will be able to do something. And both my older boys were attorneys. My older brothers were attorneys.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where did they do their go -to -law school at?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: At Washington DC.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, really?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: I went to graduate from the National Law College and the other one from George Washington.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And your parents footed the bill for them to go home?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: No, no, they worked their way through school. It sounds like it would take a lot of drive to go back, all the way back there, to go to school. Well, they had a lot of determination.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: From your parents, do you think?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Yes, from my parents and my uncle, my mother's brother. It's got everything to do with going to school.

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: And then one of my brothers was in Who's Who?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: in the West.

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: And one of my older brothers was my teacher for two years.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where, when you were in school?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: When I was still in the family. And boy, my sister and I had to behave. If we didn't, we'd get it when we got home.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Didn't want you to take, figured you'd be the first to take advantage of it, right?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Yes, yes. And he modernized the school, so the teacher before, not the teacher before him. Yes, it was a teacher before him, but he, the girl sat in one side of the room and boys on the other side.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And then he'd come around and he'd have a little pointer, and he'd point it, and he says, perfect or imperfect?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: You know, recess at noon when school was out. And if you didn't whisper to anyone, you said perfect. If you whispered to someone, you said, imperfect. And if you said, imperfect, you had to stay in.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you have to stay yourself?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Yes. And then he'd go around and he'd ask everybody around, if you whispered. And if they said, yes, you did, even if you didn't, he'd put down your hat and told a lie. And if you told three lies, you'd get a spanking, or you'd have to memorize a poem.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was that? Your brother cut that out?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Yes. He cut it out, and we had a wonderful school board. Mr. Blum was one of them. And he had the blacks in his shop, and he was very, very strong for school. And they had a new system for heating, so it circulated. And we didn't have to take the, go to the corner and dip the dipper out and drink water out of it anymore. That's what we had to do then. And when my brother, they got a fountain, and each one had their own cup. And we had our lunch buckets. We had places to put all our lunch buckets instead of putting it in the back of the room and it's freezing. It's lunch freezing. And, oh, I don't know, there's so many. And we had towels, and had towels so we could wash in the washbasin. We never had any of that before.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the chances of learning when you were going to school?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: I think the learning was very good. No, the first teacher had so many odd ways, but academically, the learning, he did teach us things. We did learn. And then I think a lot of the students wanted to. I know I did. I wanted to learn something. But we did learn. I think for the early days, the schools were adequate for that time. Of course, some schools may not have been. And then when my brother came to school, that's when we started having literary societies and school programs. Of course, he had gone on to high school where the Smokanis saw more of the modern ways.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So those were innovations at that time?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Yes, they were all innovations. Then the girls and boys were set up together, mixed up instead of just sitting on girls on one side and boys on the other.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You say this older, this teacher before, where did he come from? What was his, you know what his background was?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Because he does sound real old -fashioned. Well, I don't know his background. Now, he was old -fashioned in those ways. And then in teaching, right from the book, you did learn. In one bad habit, he'd have, yeah, this is perfect for imperfect, that telling lies business. And then he'd always believe the other fellow. That's not too good. No, no. And then another thing that I never liked, he'd have an upperclassman, ask them a question, and they couldn't answer, then he'd ask the lowerclassman. And if the lowerclassman could answer, he'd make fun of the upperclassman. And that's one thing I always vowed when I taught school, that's one thing I absolutely would not do. And I hope I never did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When did you decide that you were going to want to teach?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Ever since I was a little girl. I always wanted to teach school.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where do you think that came from?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Oh, my brother was a teacher. We had teachers in our family. And my great -grandfather taught school in Germany for 65 years. See, that was a long, long time ago. That was in the 1700s, 1800s. And I suppose that your mother, your parents, would have encouraged you to do that. Oh, yes. They wanted us to go to school. I know my mother said she never got to go to school very much. She was the oldest one in the family. The mother was sick so much, he had to stay home and take care of the children. And my dad always said he could have had lots of jobs if he could have had the schooling. And I wish you could see the books he kept. And he was road overseer. And he had some offices in the church. The bookkeeping was wonderful for just having very, very little school. He said when they went to school, the teacher had put his feet on the desk and he'd sleep. And kids would pair around. He said the boys with the grassal, the best was the best pupil. And so they knew what it meant to have some schooling.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How much opportunity was there for mixing between the young men and young women?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: I mean, the chances to get to know each other and to kind of find yourself a partner.

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Well, here it was. Of course, there was the church and the school. Then we'd have dances. And of course, then when we got older, where we could mix, we'd have dances at Leland and ball games. Kenric, we hardly ever got to Kenric, but as far as Southwick. And that was about the way they'd mix.

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Well, say like a date.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they have dating?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Oh, yes. Yes, they had dating. Of course, the boys come with the buggy and horses.

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: But I don't think they dated quite as young. I think they were a little bit older.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, to go on a date, where would you be likely to go?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Well.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would it be to someone else's house or something?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Yes, to the dances and then to the literary societies. It would be about the end.

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: There weren't so many places to go to. Of course, when I got older, then there was the, at Kenric, they finally had the movies. That's about it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When did you meet Mr.Newman?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Oh, I've known him since he, because we were children. We knew the family in Minnesota, and we knew them here. And then he went to Canada and Minnesota, and then he came back here. That's where I met him again.

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Had you been teaching already by the time that...

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Yes, met him again. Excuse me. Sure, please.

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Education, but he was a self -educated man. He learned things by himself, learned music and had an orchestra. And he did taxidermy work. He learned that all by himself. And also many, many things.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he farm?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Yes.

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Yes, he farmed.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have some teaching school when you got there?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Yes, I quit teaching. And I had an opportunity to go back to teaching during World War II, when there was a shortage of teachers. And I just, the youngest son was at home. And so I asked my husband and my son, I said, I had this opportunity to teach. He said, you've got enough teaching right here at home. So I never mentioned it again. Well, I can see too. I was needed here.

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: I understood that really women had to quit when they got married in most places. They did when I was younger, yes. If they were married, they couldn't teach. I often wondered about that. I never understood too well. It seems like sort of a discrimination in a way. It was.

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: But that was still when I was younger, that they couldn't get married. Now, if I could have taught, when I was married, they could teach when they were married.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you just chose not to?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have to do a lot of work yourself to help out on the farm then, after you were married?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Oh, no. I never did any work on the farm. No. My husband's father lived with us. And I never did work out. I never did work out in the field. I worked on making my garden and took care of my garden and things like that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But not any field were - Did you milk cows?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Yes, I milk cows.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think you did more or less what your mother had done?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Pretty much?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Quite a bit. Of course, it had a little easier, a little more modern things. But quite a bit. With the washing, we didn't have to do it by hand and so on until the electricity came. Of course, after a while, we had the machines that we turned by hand. But really, as far as easy as concerned, it wasn't easy until we got the electricity.

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Let's see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When did you get married? About what year?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was it like in the 20s for you and your husband farming? Were those rough years for you?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Well, we didn't have anything.

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: But we just worked and just took it the way they came. See, we were just renting and had debts to pay off.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did the rental work? Was it that you lived in a house that you rented?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: You lived in the house and then you paid one -third of the crop went to the owner. The renter had two -thirds. Of course, the renter paid all the expenses.

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Well, that was just about right because the owner had taxes to pay and so on had to live, too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you find that you got ahead in the 20s quite a bit, or was it really just touch and go to those years?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: The person never got ahead too far. Really, we didn't make any money. Well, we finally bought this place, and it took us a number of years to pay off the mortgage. Then we bought another 200 acres.

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Of course, we didn't have it all paid off when my husband died. When we sold it, then we sold it. In the family, kept it in the family.

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Well, I know that people told me that when the depression hit, and I don't know if it was true of you, but I've heard others say when the depression hit in the early 30s, it wasn't even much different than it had been because things had been so bad before.

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Well, that's it. We never noticed it so much because we had a hard time before. Of course, you didn't get a very good price for your things and so on, but you lived just about like you did before. You didn't buy any luxuries anyway, just the necessities.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That was the same as it happened for you in the 20s?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Oh, yes.

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: We had a lot of the necessities and worked hard, and we were happy and satisfied.

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Well, now I know that some of the reputation of some of the people around here, they've done awfully well as far as getting pretty good size spreads and all. They have.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When did that develop for them? Was that at the same time?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Well, now we've met. That's 80 acres, and then we bought 200 acres, and we earned every penny of it. But a great many of the people around here have inherited. Their fathers had some, of course, and the children were home and worked, and just got more and more, but a lot of it is inherited. The fathers and the parents had it. That's the same way as it is around Genesee. Yes, just practically the same thing. Those people that inherited from the parents had such a good start. Well, that's the way it is here, too. Well, see, my dad didn't have so much as far as money is concerned, because we all went on to school. Of course, my parents did pay for my schooling.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And your husband's father was, he didn't have a big spread either?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: No, he had very little, and he was a well -educated man, but he didn't have anything. He had absolutely nothing. He lost everything he had. He traded land here for some place in Washington and lost that, and went to Canada, and went to Canada, and he lost everything he had.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where did he go in Canada? You know, was it Alberta or something?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Yes, Alberta. It's seven persons Alberta. Uh -huh.

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: So that was, he was trying to farm on the, in that prairie, that northern country.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Had a rough time with the harvests and all?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Yes. Well, that's a common story.

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How does a lot of people run?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: I know. I should say.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, when he traded his place here, it was for land in eastern Washington?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: It was, yes, at Tae Washington. It was in this desert country.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Hey?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Yes. And they say now that's a good farming country. They see they farm different now than they did at that time. And the people that live there now are doing it real well.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he come back here, the grandfather?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: No, he went to Moscow, and then from Moscow he went to Canada. And then Canada back to Minnesota, and then he came back here.

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Well, um, I guess I'd like to ask you a little more about what the community itself around here is. What it was like when you were growing up.

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: I would imagine it was bigger.

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Yes, it was much bigger. It was very much bigger because of families. There were more families, and there were more children in the families. It used to be old six, seven, to ten children in the family. And I know at school at one time here at Cameron, there were as many as, that is in the grade school, there were as many as 60 in the school. And, of course, then finally they had to quit school here because there weren't enough children to keep school. Then they started, that was consolidation. Yes, that was consolidation with Kendrick.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did you feel about that, and the people feel about that? Were they very sorry to see the school go here?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Well, sorry in some way and glad in another.

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Sorry to see the community life gone from school because he had lots of activities at school. And then kind of glad in another.

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: I don't know how many schools there were, and there was a lot of friction there and jealousy. And you know what it did in school, and when they consolidated, everything goes down there, and they all worked towards the same thing. I think there's a better feeling all around in the consolidation.

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: That's interesting to me to say that.

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: What kind of friction, just in, you know, I don't mean any specific people. Well, maybe some, oh, this school is better, and that school is better, and then athletics, one of them, oh, a big, of course, sometimes it's just a little beyond what they should, of good judgments. And now when they're all together, they all work towards the same thing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you mean that before every little community was kind of a rival?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Yes, they were rivals. That's right.

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: And I think it gave them more opportunities. Although the little schools, I think, in their day were good at that time, because otherwise the children wouldn't have had any school.

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Well, I've heard, what I've heard against consolidation is that for some communities, it may not be true in camera, but for some, the community kind of lost its focus. These little ones, because the school was the center.

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: I think that the language was most of them, because that was the center, and that's where you'd have your activities and different things, and you don't have that now. And then there's one thing about the smaller schools, all the students could take part in things. When they go to the bigger schools, it's just the better ones that can take part in certain things. Well, there are always some things for it and some things against it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What happened to Cameron? Do you think that the community kept itself as a community after the school's consolidated?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Well, I think, well, maybe something, of course there's one thing, they have the church here, and they have activities there. So that kind of holds them together. And then they have 4 -H, and that helps with all this whole territory. Of course, it doesn't mean it isn't the camera that leave them, but they come from all these places. And I think that's one, that's another thing. And then we have the whole demonstration stuff to have for the women. So there are a few things.

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: It seems to me that the church was more important in Cameron than in a lot of places from the beginning, because I know a lot of the communities didn't get around to building a church until years after they had a school, whereas Cameron must have had a church almost as soon as they had a school. It was just about, just about.

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: And because it happened to be, because the minister happened to come here and get all his congregation, his old congregation, different people, from the same religion, I think that's one reason it was for Cameron. And then, of course, there were some other families here too, but a majority of them belonged to that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were there any families in Cameron that weren't German at the time?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: I think so, yes, there were some families here that weren't German.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How much would they take part in a community life?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Oh, not too much. That I remember, of course, it might have been more before, you know, when I, before I came here, and I was a little youngster that I don't remember.

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: I wonder if they learned how to talk German, these people who were not German.

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: No, I don't think so.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But say like in school, did you go to school with kids that weren't German?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Oh, yes.

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Yes.

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: And there's one thing I know that my sister and I, well, a lot of times at least, they'd all get together and talk German, and maybe some, I'm not, couldn't talk German. Well, we always talk English because we always tell them. It wasn't very nice for those, for those that couldn't understand, they might think we're talking about them. We always try to make them welcome. Of course, we always thought that from home. When anybody came to our house, my mother would say, they'll make a difference to who they are. You treat them nice. Because you don't have to associate with people that you don't want to, but it says if they come to your home, you'll be civil to them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How much of this visiting coming to homes really was there?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: There was a lot of it. There was just a lot of it. People would go from one home to another on Sunday.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Every Sunday?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: No, not every Sunday. Quite a few Sundays. And then it was just, they wouldn't have a great big dinner, just eat what they had.

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: I hope I can help you with something else. I hope I can help you in here now.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, you have?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Because it's really what we want to try to say as much as we can, what life is like for people, kind of similarities.

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Not early days, I used to have baseball games, I can remember. On Sundays, they'd meet and have baseball. And horseshoe pitching, used to have a lot of horseshoe pitching.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Horseshoe pitching, this would be a community affair?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: No, it wouldn't be a community just when they'd visit. And sometimes too, if they'd have these picnics and so on.

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: I was trying to think of some other things.

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: I was thinking about asking you about the kind of opportunities that girls have compared to boys in those days.

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Well, I don't know, not too much, I think. Just the only thing for girls to do is go out and do some housework or something. And they didn't have the opportunities that they have now.

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: That was common.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you do any of that?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: No. My sister and I always told my parents that we weren't going to be anybody's slaves. We'd go out and dig ditches before we'd go and be somebody's slaves. We just absolutely weren't going to do that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And why did you feel?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: I wonder why. Well, we wanted to go out of school and learn something.

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: And do something with ourselves.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did many of your friends' work as maids?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: I know Hadi Johnson did. A few. There were some, yes. Yes. Well, when we were youngsters, we didn't look up on the way we should have. We didn't want to do it. Because that's an honorable work and those people are just as honest as anybody. But we didn't want to do it. Well, to this day, I don't really like housework. I'd rather do something constructive.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But it seems like with the choices that were open to women at that time, what else could they do?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: There was nothing else they could do. There were no opportunities. Once in a while, some girls could work in the store and flirt in the store with the work. Very many stores either. There weren't any opportunities with children.

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Well, now I've heard a lot of talk about the old country ways as compared to the new country. And that in the old country, the women were looked upon more as slaves. More as they'd taken the orders and their husbands making the decisions.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you say that that's true, a difference between the old and the new country?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Yes, I think more so.

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: I think as time went on, the women had more to say. Although in our household, my mother had as much to say as my dad.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Really, ever since you were a kid?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Yes, I can remember, yes.

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: They shared the responsibility of decisions. Oh, yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They made decisions together?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He didn't just make them for her?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Mm -mm.

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Well, they were made together. They were made separately. The other one heard about it, so they worked together.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think it was a little unusual?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Yes, more so. I think so. I think at first the men did have more. The woman was supposed to be in the house and do the housework, and the man was supposed to be the boss. I think that was quite a bit of the attitude.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think your father minded to have his wife making decisions?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Oh, no. No, no. No, he was very satisfied with it. That's good. Yes, because he really worshiped my mother.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that you?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: No, hardly me.

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Well, that's good, because I had that idea that it was kind of different, and that that's one of the reasons that, I don't know if that's true, but it seemed like one of the reasons that girls didn't have the opportunities was because of that old idea. Yes. I don't know if that's...

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Yes, and then, well, you know, they didn't go to school and things, or go and look out for work, or they didn't go to school.

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: There weren't many opportunities for girls at that time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they treat in your family, did they treat the girls differently than the boys as far as what you could and couldn't do?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: No.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Your boys didn't have more independence than the girls?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Oh, no. No, no more.

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: No, that was the same.

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Now, that's different, too, than a lot of the girls, because I've had a lot of people tell me that the girls worked, just didn't have the freedom that the brothers did. No, we did. We did. They were just about...one was like the other.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But what about your friends, your girlfriends?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Well, I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know, when you're young, what's that?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: You don't pay too much attention. But it seems to me that they...it was just about the same at most places, like it was at home, as much as I can remember. I think most of the people treated their boys and girls alike. Because I know when I went on to high school, my dad was telling me so many.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I said, why in the world do you send a girl to school?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: He said, girl, you don't need to go to school. And there were quite a few of them said, I wish they could send theirs. So, you see, there was... Quite a few of the people said they wish they could send their girls to school.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But others said, why do you send them? Why do you send them?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Because girls who never do anything but get married anyway. Well, my dad said, they do get married. They can use it in their married life just the same as anywhere else.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, do you think that it's better now as far as the opportunities that women have compared to what it was?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Oh, yes.

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Much, much better.

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Because women have opportunities to get in most any kind of work now. And they couldn't years ago.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: If you could have had more of a career, would you have wanted to, you think?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: No, I think I'd still want to go to teaching. I like teaching, especially the little fellows. The little children. And I still love the little children now. But still, you felt that you had to quit when you got married. And nowadays, of course, there's women teach marriage. Yes. No. Well, years ago, when you were married, my husband was primary. Well, I probably had to go away from home, because we didn't have cars in those days. And if a person could get back and forth, why it'd probably be different. And now, well, the life is all together different now. Maybe some of it is for the better and some of it isn't so good.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you think wouldn't be as good now as it used to be?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Well, maybe too much freedom. I don't know. It depends upon the individual, I think.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think there's as much neighboring?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: No, not near as much neighboring. I... No, because, well, one thing, the television. People stay home for that. And you have the cars, you have opportunities to go farther away from home. And probably have to go quite a ways to get certain things that are really worthwhile. You know, maybe concerts and different things. And to learn. And to enjoy themselves.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you were married, did you and your husband share a responsibility too, or the decisions?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Yes, we always did. We always did.

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Of course, we didn't always agree, but then we could come to an agreement by talking things over.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you go together for very long? Was there courtships in those days?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Yeah, we went together for about a year.

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Years and a little over. Of course, I had known him all my life.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were you married in the church here?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: No, we were married at Lewiston.

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: I was going to ask you about play. When you were a kid, what sorts of things did you do for... You know, what girls play was.

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Oh, let's see. Of course, we always played with dolls. And...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did we play?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: I'm just trying to think.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mean, when we got older or when we were just little?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Oh, both, really. When we got older, oh, we'd do some fancy work and we'd play... We'd just board and we'd play cards and we'd play carols.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Carols?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Yes, that's a big board. And checkerboard and carols. It's like you shoot with a little... Things like that. We'd play ball. We'd play a lot of baseball.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Girls too? With the boys or just with the girls?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: With the boys. I played with the boys in eighth grade. They didn't have enough boys, so I had to play with them. I'm my girlfriend.

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: And see, my older brother, my brother's played on baseball teams. And I'd have to play with them, my brother, next to me. I'd always have to pitch for him. And I love baseball anyway.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were there games with, say, like Cameron and Leland?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Yes, yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it a hot rivalry?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Oh, quite a bit. I know, Kendrick and Juliet are really hot ones. Yes, quite a bit. I don't think it was quite as bad as Kendrick and Juliet. Of course, maybe there weren't quite as many people either, young people. And that's the way it used to be if the schools, too. Basketball games used to be very much a rivalry.

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: I know my, because my nurses all played.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they have these play party games as a part of the literaries and the school doings? What do you mean, have plays?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Play parties, I was thinking. The games like Happy's and the Miller Boy. Oh, yes, yes. We used to play that.

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: And, oh, Spin the Bottle.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Spin the Bottle? Yes, and, oh, what is that?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: So, Skip to My Lou and, oh, ...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was Spin the Bottle a kissing game?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: No, it was a, you'd Spin the Bottle and whoever, you'd ask a question or something, you'd point to them and it'd be that and then they'd have to ask a question.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were there forfeits?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: No, again, not with the Spin the Bottle, but with some of them, we'd play forfeits. We used to play, and, hmm, I'm trying to think of some of the games now, I've kind of forgotten. It's been quite a while since we played them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, Rolande, what was it?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: Telephone was another one we played.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever play it?

speaker: Ida Mielke Newman
words: No. One of them had to start whispering and then they'd have to pass it on.
